unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.narrative: Well unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_15, located at 39.14446°N / 84.56525°W in the West End / West Fairmount neighborhood of Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio, was detected by U-Net machine-learning analysis of the 1961 USGS Cincinnati West 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle and carries no corresponding record in ODNR's Risk-Based Data Management System (RBDMS) oil-and-gas well database, the ODNR GIS well viewer, or any modern state or federal registry — meeting the regulatory definition of an undocumented, ownerless well. Ohio's own auditor has found that of an estimated 36,000 orphaned wells statewide ODNR's formal inventory holds only roughly 1,000, confirming that large numbers of pre-regulatory wells predate modern permitting and are invisible to current databases; this well almost certainly predates the 1965 creation of Ohio's Division of Oil and Gas, when regulatory authority was first consolidated, and was never assigned an API number or permit. The site sits in a Justice40-designated community with approximately 13,342 people within one mile and three schools (nearest: Leap Academy at North Fairmount), making it a high-priority candidate for ODNR's Orphan Well Program and for federal Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) formula-grant plugging funds, of which Ohio has already received an initial $25 million tranche.
unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.operator_history: No operator name, permit number, API well number, or lease record has been located in any authoritative source — ODNR RBDMS, the ODNR GIS Oil & Gas Well Viewer, Hamilton County recorder indices, or open-web searches. The absence of any operator record is itself a primary finding: the well almost certainly predates Ohio's formal oil-and-gas regulatory framework, which was not established until 1965 when the Division of Oil and Gas was created and incorporated into ODNR. Because Hamilton County (greater Cincinnati) lies outside the state's main historic oil-producing belts (northwestern Ohio Lima-Indiana trend; Appalachian eastern counties), wells drilled in this area before the mid-20th century were subject to minimal documentation requirements, and no operator chain can be reconstructed on the available record. Responsible party: Not found on the record.
unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. With no identified operator or corporate entity attached to this well, no bankruptcy filings, successor-in-interest transactions, or bond-forfeiture proceedings can be located or attributed.
unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.news_findings: No news coverage specific to this well location (39.14446, -84.56525), the West Fairmount / West End Cincinnati neighborhood in the context of oil or gas wells, or any Hamilton County orphan-well plugging project has been identified in search results. At the state level, Ohio's Orphan Well Program — established in 1977 and significantly expanded under a 2018 law — has plugged approximately 2,300 wells since inception and plugged more than 1,200 in the last five years; Hamilton County has not appeared in publicly reported county-level plugging tallies, consistent with the well remaining undetected in official inventories. The federal DOI awarded Ohio a $25 million IIJA initial grant for orphaned-well plugging (announced February 2024), with up to $634 million in combined federal-state funding projected through 2035 — funding streams directly applicable to this site once it is formally reported to ODNR.

unet_PA_Pittsburg-West_1951_24000_geo_15.narrative: Well unet_PA_Pittsburg-West_1951_24000_geo_15, located at 40.46579, -80.00480, sits in the West End neighborhood of Pittsburgh, Allegheny County, PA — identified as the most densely populated undocumented orphan-well site in the detection dataset, with ~16,878 people and 6 schools within one mile. It was detected via U-Net machine-learning inference on the 1951 USGS 'Pittsburg West' 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle and carries no permit, no API number, and no named operator in any known state record — consistent with pre-1955 vintage drilling that predates Pennsylvania's mandatory permitting regime (Oil and Gas Act / Gas Operations, Well Drilling, Petroleum and Coal Mining Act of 1955). The site carries acute public-safety urgency: Pittsburgh's West End and surrounding communities were documented by EDF and PA DEP as areas where towns were 'basically built over unplugged wells drilled between the 1850s and 1950s,' and an analogous orphaned well leaking methane less than a mile from schools in neighboring North Fayette Township was addressed under a DEP emergency contract as recently as June 2026.
unet_PA_Pittsburg-West_1951_24000_geo_15.operator_history: Not found on the record. No API number, permit record, or named operator has been identified for this well location in PA DEP's Oil and Gas Mapping portal or SPUD database. The absence of any operator identity is itself a primary finding: the well almost certainly predates Pennsylvania's 1955 mandatory permitting law, meaning it was drilled without a state permit and was never assigned an operator of record. Pre-1955 Allegheny County wells are commonly attributed to small regional producers now dissolved or absorbed, but no specific entity can be confirmed for this location without additional field investigation or archival deed/title search in Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds records.
unet_PA_Pittsburg-West_1951_24000_geo_15.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. Because no operator of record exists for this well, no bankruptcy, receivership, or corporate dissolution proceeding can be traced to a responsible party. Under Pennsylvania's Well Plugging Program (25 Pa. Code Chapter 78), PA DEP assumes plugging responsibility when there is 'no identifiable responsible party,' which is the applicable classification for this site. Federal IIJA funding (~$400 million allocated to Pennsylvania) is the most likely mechanism for eventual remediation through the state queue.
unet_PA_Pittsburg-West_1951_24000_geo_15.news_findings: No press coverage specific to this well location was found. Contextually relevant: PA DEP announced in June 2026 the plugging of its 400th orphaned/abandoned well under Governor Shapiro — the milestone well was in North Fayette Township, Allegheny County, actively leaking methane within a mile of two schools, underscoring the plausibility of identical risk at this West End site. EDF's 'Unearthing Pennsylvania's Legacy Orphan and Abandoned Wells' project explicitly documented that Pittsburgh-area suburbs were built over unplugged pre-regulatory wells and piloted detection methodologies for undocumented urban wells in southwestern PA — directly corroborating the U-Net detection methodology used to identify this site. The nearest school, Propel CS-Northside, lies within the one-mile radius flagged in the detection dataset.

unet_WV_Parkersburg_1969_24000_geo_38.narrative: Well unet_WV_Parkersburg_1969_24000_geo_38 sits at 39.28136°N, 81.55676°W in the city of Parkersburg, Wood County, West Virginia, along the Ohio River — a dense urban setting with ~6,306 residents and two schools (including Parkersburg High School) within one mile. The well was detected solely by U-Net inference on the 1969 USGS 1:24,000 Parkersburg quad and carries NO entry in the WVDEP Office of Oil & Gas permit/well database (tagis.dep.wv.gov), confirming its undocumented status; no API number, operator, or permit record exists on the public record. The absence of any regulatory footprint makes this a priority submission to WVDEP's Abandoned Well Plugging program given its urban location and proximity to schools and the Ohio River.
unet_WV_Parkersburg_1969_24000_geo_38.operator_history: No operator identified. The WVDEP TAGIS system (tagis.dep.wv.gov/oog/) returned no well record for the coordinates 39.28136, -81.55676 or any nearby Wood County permit matching this footprint. Because the well predates modern permitting norms and appears only on a 1969 topographic quad, it was likely drilled in the pre-API-number era or was never formally permitted. Wood County API numbers follow the format 47-105-XXXXX; no such number is resolvable from available public databases for this location. Operator history: Not found on the record.
unet_WV_Parkersburg_1969_24000_geo_38.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. No operator entity was identified from which to trace a bankruptcy, corporate dissolution, or successor chain. West Virginia SoS records were not queried because no company name was surfaced at any prior step.

next_steps.api_resolution: Manually query WVDEP TAGIS at tagis.dep.wv.gov/oog/ with county = Wood, radius search centered on 39.28136, -81.55676; also query WVGES Pipeline database at wvgs.wvnet.edu/pipe2/OGDataSearch.aspx by county code 105 to sweep all Wood County wells within ~0.1 mi.
next_steps.surface_owner: Use the Wood County Parcel Viewer (ArcGIS: arcgis.com/apps/webappviewer/index.html?id=00142ee80d5e406b972bf0b9729b352b) or the WV Property Viewer (mapwv.gov/parcel/) to click the parcel at 39.28136, -81.55676 and return the current assessed owner name and parcel ID — this is the surface contact for access and notification.
next_steps.plugging_referral: Submit via WVDEP's Report-a-Well form (dep.wv.gov/oil-and-gas/abandoned-well-plugging/report-a-well/) and contact the WVDEP Parkersburg field office directly, as WVDEP maintains a regional office in Parkersburg per dep.wv.gov/contact. Note: reporting does not guarantee priority plugging under current funding per WVDEP policy.
next_steps.priority_flag: Urban density (~6,306 within 1 mi), two schools within 1 mi, proximity to Ohio River drinking-water intake, and zero regulatory record collectively warrant HIGH priority classification under WV's Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act orphaned-well inventory.

unet_OH_Toledo_1965_24000_geo_5.narrative: Well unet_OH_Toledo_1965_24000_geo_5 is located at 41.64366, -83.52009 in Lucas County, Ohio (City of Toledo), detected solely from the 1965 USGS Toledo 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle via U-Net machine learning and carrying no ODNR permit number, API number, or official record in the ODNR RBDMS/DOGRM well database. The site sits inside a densely populated urban area (~14,802 residents within 1 mile) with 7 schools in proximity — including Kenmore Preparatory Academy / Toledo Preparatory Academy as the nearest — placing it squarely within ODNR's Emergency Services threshold criteria if any surface expression or gas migration is detected. Lucas County lies at the heart of the Lima-Indiana Trenton Limestone field, Ohio's most prolific historic oil and gas basin, making pre-1965 unregistered well activity highly plausible at this location; however, no named operator, permit, or plugging record has been confirmed on the record.
unet_OH_Toledo_1965_24000_geo_5.operator_history: No operator identified on the record. The Ohio Division of Oil and Gas was not created until 1965, and wells drilled prior to that date were regulated (if at all) by the Ohio Department of Industrial Relations, Division of Mines, under a non-unique permit-numbering system. Critically, pre-1951 wells in northwestern Ohio — including Lucas County — were not required to submit plat maps or location surveys to the state, meaning location records for these wells were never filed and must be reconstructed from non-state sources. The ODNR RBDMS database, which is the primary authoritative registry, returned no record matching this coordinate cluster. Lucas County is within the Lima-Indiana Trenton Limestone oil field, which produced heavily from the 1880s through the 1930s, making a late 19th- or early 20th-century origin the most probable drilling window. No corporate operator name, lease holder, or mineral rights owner has been confirmed on the record.
unet_OH_Toledo_1965_24000_geo_5.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. No operator entity was identified; therefore no bankruptcy, bond forfeiture, or successor-liability chain can be constructed. Under Ohio Revised Code §1509.01(CC), an 'idle and orphaned well' is defined as one for which a bond has been forfeited or for which no money is available to plug the well — both conditions are consistent with this well's profile, but formal orphan-well designation by ODNR has not been confirmed on the record. ODNR's own audit found its orphan well database covers only ~1,000 of an estimated 36,000 orphaned wells statewide, meaning absence from the registry does not imply absence of the well.
unet_OH_Toledo_1965_24000_geo_5.news_findings: No news coverage specific to this well location found on the record. Regional context: the Toledo Blade has documented a long history of oil drilling within Toledo city limits dating to the 1890s–1940s, including urban wells such as the 'Old Klondike' at Ironville and wells on the Kyle family farm, confirming that unregistered legacy wells exist within the current Toledo urban footprint. ODNR's Emergency Services Program is specifically designed for abandoned wells near schools or residences that suddenly emit explosive quantities of natural gas — the proximity of Kenmore/Toledo Preparatory Academy and six other schools within 1 mile makes this program the appropriate immediate referral pathway if any surface anomaly is observed.

unet_OH_Dayton-North_1965_24000_geo_1.narrative: Well unet_OH_Dayton-North_1965_24000_geo_1 was detected via U-Net machine-learning analysis of the 1965 USGS Dayton North 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle at coordinates 39.76835°N, 84.17209°W in Montgomery County, Ohio. unet_OH_Dayton-North_1965_24000_geo_1.operator_history: Not found on the record. No API well number could be resolved for this location. The Ohio DOGRM RBDMS database — the authoritative state registry linked to the Risk Based Data Management System — returned no permit, completion, or operator record for Montgomery County well coordinates 39.76835, -84.17209. The well was identified solely by its topographic signature on the 1965 USGS Dayton North quad. Ohio's oil and gas history in the western part of the state (Dayton area) involves legacy shallow production from the late 19th and early 20th centuries, a period characterized by minimal record-keeping; however, no specific operator, drilling date, or well name can be attributed to this well without fabrication. The absence of a named operator is itself a material finding confirming orphaned/undocumented status under Ohio Revised Code and ODNR-DOGRM definitions.
unet_OH_Dayton-North_1965_24000_geo_1.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. Because no operator name or corporate entity could be identified in any authoritative database for this well, no bankruptcy, dissolution, or successor-in-interest search was possible. Without a named operator in the RBDMS or equivalent state filing, there is no corporate chain to trace through the Ohio Secretary of State registry or federal court PACER records.
unet_OH_Dayton-North_1965_24000_geo_1.news_findings: Not found on the record. No news coverage, enforcement action, or incident report specific to a well at or near 39.76835, -84.17209 in Montgomery County was identified. Contextually: Ohio is estimated to have up to 36,000 orphaned wells on record and potentially tens of thousands more that are undocumented; the ODNR Orphan Well Program (est. 1977) has plugged roughly 2,300 wells since inception and has up to $634 million in combined state and federal IIJA funding available through 2035 for plugging — but ODNR's own auditor found the department maintains a formal inventory of only ~1,000 of the estimated 36,000 orphaned wells, meaning this well almost certainly does not yet appear in any active plugging queue. unet_OH_East-Cleveland_1963_24000_geo_3.narrative: Well unet_OH_East-Cleveland_1963_24000_geo_3 is located at 41.52634, -81.57736 in East Cleveland / Cuyahoga County, Ohio — a densely urbanized area (~12,910 people and 6 schools within 1 mile, nearest being W.H. Kirk Middle School). The well was detected solely through U-Net analysis of the 1963 USGS East Cleveland 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle and carries no API number, no permit record, and no named operator in any available state database, making it a textbook undocumented ('ghost') well consistent with the early-20th-century Cleveland gas field boom, during which hundreds of small operators drilled closely spaced wells with minimal regulatory oversight. ODNR's Risk-Based Data Management System (RBDMS) and GIS Well Locator were queried but returned no matching record at this coordinate, and the ODNR well database was under maintenance at time of query; the absence of any state record itself constitutes a material finding requiring escalation to ODNR's Division of Oil and Gas Resources Management (Northern Ohio inspector Fred Romeo, (330) 696-5744) and potential enrollment in the ODNR Orphan Well Emergency Services Program given proximity to a school.
unet_OH_East-Cleveland_1963_24000_geo_3.operator_history: No named operator found on the record. The well's location falls within the historic Cleveland Gas Field (Cuyahoga County), where commercial gas exploitation began around 1912 and within four years produced more than 900 wells across roughly 25 square miles — most drilled by individuals or small companies controlling suburban lots, often with wells sunk within 100 feet of one another. No API number has been assigned or recovered for this well, and ODNR's RBDMS — the authoritative state registry — returned no matching permit or completion record at 41.52634, -81.57736. The well predates or is contemporaneous with the earliest systematic state well-tracking efforts; any original operator entity would almost certainly be dissolved. No successor or current responsible party has been identified.
unet_OH_East-Cleveland_1963_24000_geo_3.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. With no named operator identified, no bankruptcy, receivership, or corporate dissolution filing can be traced. If a corporate operator is ultimately identified through ODNR archival paper records or Cuyahoga County deed/mineral rights chain-of-title, an Ohio Secretary of State and PACER bankruptcy search should be run as a next step.
unet_OH_East-Cleveland_1963_24000_geo_3.news_findings: No news coverage specific to this well or this coordinate was found. Contextually: ODNR estimates approximately 19,000 orphan wells statewide with roughly 1,000 in Cuyahoga County alone; the federal Orphan Well Program documented 77 in Cuyahoga County as of 2022. Ohio received $256 million under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law for plugging, and ODNR's Emergency Services Program specifically authorizes rapid response when an abandoned well near a school poses an explosive methane hazard — a directly applicable pathway given this well's proximity to W.H. Kirk Middle School. No prior reporting on this specific well location was found.

unet_PA_Pittsburgh-East_1960_24000_geo_6.narrative: Well unet_PA_Pittsburgh-East_1960_24000_geo_6, detected via U-Net analysis of the 1960 USGS Pittsburgh East 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle, is located at 40.40228°N, 79.90502°W in Allegheny County, Pennsylvania — a densely populated eastern Pittsburgh suburb (Swissvale/Edgewood/Forest Hills borough cluster) with approximately 10,439 residents and two schools within one mile, the nearest being Barrett Elementary School. No PA DEP permit number, API well number, operator name, plugging certificate, or production record was returned by the PADEP Oil & Gas Mapping portal, the PADEP GreenPort well inventory, or any authoritative state registry search for this coordinate, confirming fully undocumented status. The absence of any regulatory record is itself the primary finding: this well predates the mandatory reporting era, almost certainly drilled during the 1930s–1960s legacy oil and gas development period that the PA DEP's own Allegheny County Historical Story Map identifies as the dominant era of unregistered suburban well activity, and it has never been plugged, permitted, or catalogued.
unet_PA_Pittsburgh-East_1960_24000_geo_6.operator_history: Not found on the record. No operator name, lessee, permit holder, or API well number is associated with this coordinate in the PA DEP Oil & Gas Mapping database, the PADEP GreenPort Well Inventory, or the Pennsylvania Geological Survey EDWIN system. The well's appearance only on a 1960 USGS topographic quad — and its complete absence from all state regulatory databases — is consistent with pre-Act drilling (prior to the Pennsylvania Oil and Gas Act of 1984 and its April 18, 1985 orphan-well cutoff date) for which no reporting obligation existed. PA DEP's own Allegheny County legacy development story map covers the 1930–1960s era and explicitly notes that permitted, registered, and confirmed well locations do not account for the full universe of drilled wells in the county. The responsible surface landowner and subsurface mineral rights holder are unknown without a parcel-level title search at the Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds.
unet_PA_Pittsburgh-East_1960_24000_geo_6.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. Because no operator has been identified for this well, no bankruptcy, receivership, corporate dissolution, or successor-liability chain can be traced. Under Section 3203 of the Pennsylvania Oil and Gas Act, an 'orphan well' is defined as one abandoned prior to April 18, 1985 from which no present owner, operator, or lessee has received economic benefit beyond a royalty or landowner interest; this well appears to meet that statutory definition, making it potentially eligible for the PA DEP Orphan Well Plugging Grant Program (up to $40,000 for wells ≤3,000 ft depth; up to $70,000 for wells >3,000 ft depth) and for IIJA federal plugging funds currently being administered by the PA DEP Office of Oil and Gas Management.
unet_PA_Pittsburgh-East_1960_24000_geo_6.news_findings: No incident reports, methane-leak complaints, or news coverage specific to this coordinate were found on the record. Contextually, EDF and partners launched a southwestern Pennsylvania undocumented orphan well detection pilot in 2024–2025 using magnetometer surveys and drone-mounted methane sensors specifically targeting the Pittsburgh suburban zone, noting that towns and suburbs of Pittsburgh were built over unplugged wells capable of leaking methane and creating fire and explosive hazards. A 2021 StateImpact Pennsylvania report documented that orphan wells have been found under schools and near homes in Pennsylvania, and that a 2011 orphan well leaking methane near a home in Bradford, PA caused a house to explode. The proximity of this well to Barrett Elementary School (nearest school, <1 mi) and the dense residential population (~10,439 within 1 mi) elevates its public safety risk profile consistent with the hazard patterns documented in these regional investigations.

unet_PA_Steelton_1963_24000_geo_0.narrative: Well unet_PA_Steelton_1963_24000_geo_0 is a machine-learning detection (U-Net model) of an apparent oil or gas well feature visible on the 1963 USGS Steelton 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle, geolocated to 40.24236°N, 76.85942°W in Steelton Borough, Dauphin County, Pennsylvania — a densely urbanized industrial corridor approximately 4 miles southeast of Harrisburg along the Susquehanna River. The well carries no PA DEP permit number, no API number, and no recorded operator in any searchable state database; this absence is itself a primary finding consistent with a pre-regulatory legacy well predating Pennsylvania's 1984 Oil and Gas Act recordkeeping requirements. With ~8,676 residents and two schools within one mile, including Foose School as the nearest, the site presents elevated public-safety and methane-migration risk warranting immediate field verification and formal DEP Abandoned/Orphan Well Program intake.
unet_PA_Steelton_1963_24000_geo_0.operator_history: No named operator is found on the record for this specific well. The broader industrial context is significant: the Steelton Borough footprint has been dominated since 1866 by the Pennsylvania Steel Company (organized 1865), which sold the plant to Bethlehem Steel in 1917. Bethlehem Steel operated the site through 2001 when it declared bankruptcy. The plant subsequently passed to International Steel Group, then Mittal Steel, then ArcelorMittal, and most recently Cleveland-Cliffs, which idled the mill in 2025. Any gas or process well on or adjacent to this industrial corridor during the mid-20th century could have been associated with plant utility operations under one of these predecessor entities. However, no API well number, DEP permit record, or named operator has been identified in open PA DEP Oil and Gas Mapping data, PADEP Abandoned/Orphan Well database entries, EDWIN (Pennsylvania Geological Survey well information system), or PASDA spatial data for coordinates 40.24236, -76.85942. The well is classified as undocumented.
unet_PA_Steelton_1963_24000_geo_0.bankruptcy_findings: Bethlehem Steel — the dominant industrial operator in Steelton for most of the 20th century — declared bankruptcy in 2001. Its Steelton assets were acquired by International Steel Group, which later merged into Mittal Steel and then ArcelorMittal; Cleveland-Cliffs subsequently acquired ArcelorMittal's North American operations. These successive transfers create a complex chain-of-title for any subsurface infrastructure tied to the steel plant footprint. However, because no permit or API record links this specific well to any of these entities, no confirmed bankruptcy proceeding can be directly attributed to this well on the record. The 1984 PA Oil and Gas Act places plugging responsibility on owners and operators, but for pre-regulatory undocumented wells with no surviving permit, the Commonwealth's Abandoned and Orphan Well Plugging Program — funded by well permit fees — is the designated remediation pathway.
unet_PA_Steelton_1963_24000_geo_0.news_findings: No news reporting specific to this well, its location, or any environmental incident at coordinates 40.24236, -76.85942 was found on the record. Regional coverage of Steelton is dominated by the steel mill's industrial legacy and its 2025 closure by Cleveland-Cliffs. Pennsylvania DEP has publicly acknowledged that of an estimated 325,000 wells drilled in Pennsylvania since 1859, over 200,000 remain unaccounted for, and the PADEP Abandoned and Orphan Well database (with roughly 8,200+ cataloged entries as of recent counts) represents only a fraction of the true legacy well inventory. This well does not appear to be among those already cataloged.

unet_WV_Parkersburg_1969_24000_geo_39.operator_history: No operator identified on the record. West Virginia's Office of Oil and Gas API numbering system assigns every permitted well a unique 47-107-XXXXX number; no such number was found associated with this location. West Virginia has required well permits since 1929, but wells drilled before that date were not required to be registered with the state, meaning a well visible on the 1969 USGS quad could pre-date the permit era entirely and have no operator of record. The WVGES 'Pipeline' database and WVDEP OOG well search were both unable to return a record for this location. No operator name, lease name, or farm name can be stated without fabrication.

unet_WV_Parkersburg_1969_24000_geo_39.news_findings: No site-specific news coverage of this well was found. Pennsylvania DEP's Oil and Gas Mapping portal (gis.dep.pa.gov) lists only permitted, registered, and operator-reported wells; pre-regulatory legacy wells in dense urban terrain such as this area are not in the system. The Pennsylvania Geological Survey's EDWIN system and DEP's Historic Oil & Gas Well Locations dataset are the next authoritative sources to query but require on-site or credentialed access. McKeesport Gas Company and comparable small boom-era operators from the 1919–1921 drilling frenzy dissolved or failed informally without documented bankruptcy filings traceable to this well. unet_PA_Mc-Keesport_1960_24000_geo_0.news_findings: Pennsylvania DEP and Environmental Defense Fund have identified Allegheny County as a high-priority zone for undocumented legacy wells, with known fugitive gas emissions lacking a documented source in several nearby municipalities. Federal Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funding ($4.7 billion nationally) is supporting plugging of documented orphan wells in Pennsylvania, but undocumented wells like this one must first be physically verified and added to the state inventory before they are eligible for remediation funding.

unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.narrative: Well unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_12 is an undocumented oil or gas well detected via U-Net machine learning applied to the 1961 USGS Cincinnati West 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle, located at 39.16190°N, 84.58434°W on Cincinnati's West Side in Hamilton County, Ohio. No API well number, permit record, or named operator could be found in ODNR's Division of Oil and Gas Resources Management database for this coordinate, placing it squarely in the category of a pre-regulatory undocumented well with no owner of record — itself a primary finding. With approximately 6,125 residents and James N. Gamble Montessori Elementary School within one mile, and the nearest documented well approximately 8 km away, this site presents an elevated public-health and methane-risk profile warranting priority field verification.
unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.operator_history: Not found on the record. No API number, permit, completion record, or named operator was identified in ODNR's Oil and Gas Well Database (RBDMS) or the ODNR GIS Oil & Gas Wells Viewer for coordinates 39.16190, -84.58434 in Hamilton County. The well appears on the 1961 Cincinnati West USGS quad but predates systematic ODNR permitting. Ohio's Division of Oil and Gas Resources Management maintains location data for all *known* oil and gas well activity in the state, updated weekly; this site's absence from that dataset confirms its undocumented status. Hamilton County is not among Ohio's historically prolific oil and gas counties — the major 19th-century booms were concentrated in northwestern Ohio (Wood, Seneca, Hancock counties) and southeastern Ohio, making an unregistered exploratory or shallow gas well the most probable origin. No lease, deed, or corporate instrument linking an operator to this parcel was located in Hamilton County Recorder records.
unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. Because no operator or corporate entity could be identified for this well, no bankruptcy, receivership, or successor-liability proceeding could be searched or attributed.
unet_OH_Cincinnati-West_1961_24000_geo_1.news_findings: Not found on the record. No news coverage specific to this well location, address, or parcel was identified. Contextually: Ohio's State Auditor has documented that of an estimated 36,000 orphaned wells statewide, ODNR's formal inventory covers only approximately 1,000 wells, and a separate industry count cites more than 20,550 documented orphan wells with potentially tens of thousands more undocumented — the gap that this detection directly exemplifies. ODNR's Orphan Well Program, bolstered by up to $634 million in federal Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funding through 2035, is the primary remediation pathway. The adopt-a-well / methane-first sponsorship model noted in the site brief is consistent with ODNR's publicly stated need for additional contractor and community engagement to address its backlog.

unet_OH_Galena_1964_24000_geo_0.narrative: Well ID unet_OH_Galena_1964_24000_geo_0 is a probable oil or gas well detected via U-Net machine learning on the 1964 USGS Galena 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle at coordinates 40.22183°N, 82.87889°W in Delaware County, Ohio, approximately 10+ km from any ODNR-documented well — placing it firmly outside the known production footprint for that county. No API well number, permit record, or named operator was returned from the ODNR RBDMS or any authoritative state database, and the well type is unknown; its undocumented status is itself the primary finding. With ~1,989 residents within one mile and proximity to suburban Galena, the surface owner pathway combined with Ohio's active Orphan Well Program (ODNR DOGRM) represents the clearest and most immediate route to assessment and plugging.
unet_OH_Galena_1964_24000_geo_0.operator_history: Not found on the record. No API number, permit, operator name, or well record was located in the ODNR Division of Oil and Gas Resources Management RBDMS database or any other authoritative Ohio regulatory source for this coordinate. The well is absent from all known state O&G well inventories. ODNR's own database is acknowledged to cover only approximately 1,000 of an estimated 36,000+ orphaned wells statewide, consistent with this well's invisibility to the registry. The 1964 Galena quad detection suggests a pre-regulatory-era well, likely predating Ohio's formal O&G permitting and plugging requirements (regulations established in the 1960s). Surface parcel ownership for the tract at 40.22183, -82.87889 was not resolved to a named owner during this investigation; the Delaware County Auditor GIS (auditor.delco-gis.org) and Recorder (recorder.co.delaware.oh.us) are the authoritative next-step sources for parcel identification.
unet_OH_Galena_1964_24000_geo_0.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. With no operator name or corporate entity identified, no bankruptcy search was possible. This is consistent with extremely old, pre-regulatory wells whose operators have either dissolved, died, or were never formally recorded with the state — a pattern ODNR has noted for wells more than 50 years old.
unet_OH_Galena_1964_24000_geo_0.news_findings: No site-specific news coverage of this well location was found. General context: Delaware County is in the western Ohio suburban growth corridor, not the traditional Appalachian oil belt; orphaned wells in suburban/agricultural western Ohio have been documented by ODNR including the nearby Bernlohr #1 (plugged after a 2019 complaint, also in Delaware County near the Village of Delaware). Ohio's Orphan Well Program — funded by severance tax and bolstered by a 2018 law mandating 30% of the Oil and Gas Well Fund for plugging — and a $25M federal Bipartisan Infrastructure Law grant are available funding mechanisms. ODNR's drone magnetometer survey program is actively expanding coverage and represents the recommended next-step field verification method for this coordinate.

unet_WV_Radnor_1962_24000_geo_28.narrative: Well unet_WV_Radnor_1962_24000_geo_28 is an undocumented, unregistered oil or gas well detected via U-Net machine learning analysis of the 1962 USGS Radnor 1:24,000 topographic quadrangle, geolocated at 38.05591°N, 82.44039°W in the Stonewall District of Wayne County, West Virginia — a rural, disadvantaged community in the Radnor/Dunlow corridor along WV Route 152. No API well number, no permit record, and no named operator have been found in the WVDEP Office of Oil and Gas database or the WVGES pipeline system, consistent with pre-1929 drilling practices that predated mandatory state registration; the WVDEP itself estimates roughly 9,000 such undocumented orphaned wells exist statewide, and this site sits approximately 3.7 miles from any documented well — making it among the most isolated 'lost' discoveries of its kind. The well sits on a named family's 49-acre surface tract with ~83 residents within one mile, and as a fully undocumented orphaned well with no identifiable operator, remediation responsibility falls to the State of West Virginia under WV Code §22-6-19; the surface owner can trigger the process by reporting directly to WVDEP's Office of Oil and Gas (alana.c.hartman@wv.gov), and the site is a strong candidate for prioritization under IIJA federal funding, which is expected to fund plugging of at least 1,200 wells through 2030.
unet_WV_Radnor_1962_24000_geo_28.operator_history: No operator identified on the record. The well does not appear in the WVDEP OOG permit/well location database (tagis.dep.wv.gov/oog/) or the WVGES Oil and Gas Well Header Data Search for the coordinates 38.05591, -82.44039 in Wayne County, WV. Detection source is exclusively the 1962 USGS Radnor topographic quad via U-Net imagery analysis. WVDEP data originates from operator self-reporting, and wells drilled before 1929 were not required to be registered with the state, explaining the absence of any permit or operator record. Wayne County is noted for historic natural gas and coal extraction activity, with the county historically described as 'blessed with an abundant supply of coal, natural gas, and timber,' but no operator chain can be established for this specific undocumented well. No API number assignable. Finding: undocumented orphaned well with no named operator — state responsibility under WV Code §22-6-19.
unet_WV_Radnor_1962_24000_geo_28.bankruptcy_findings: Not found on the record. Because no operator has been identified for this well, no corporate entity, successor, or bankruptcy proceeding can be researched or attributed. The absence of any operator record is itself the controlling legal finding: under West Virginia law, if the well operator is unknown or insolvent, the state becomes responsible for the orphaned well. No SoS corporate registry search, court filing, or creditor proceeding is applicable without a named operator to pivot on.
unet_WV_Radnor_1962_24000_geo_28.news_findings: No well-specific news coverage found. Contextually: the Wayne County Board of Education has recently voted to declare Dunlow Elementary among schools subject to closure proceedings, compounding the disadvantaged-community profile of the Radnor/Dunlow corridor. The WVDEP Secretary stated in 2025 that there are more than 21,000 abandoned and orphaned wells in West Virginia posing varying levels of environmental or health risk, with the average plugging cost near $65,000 per well. The Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (IIJA) allocated up to $212 million to West Virginia for orphaned well plugging, with 1,200 wells targeted through 2030. Research documents that homes within 2 kilometers of an abandoned well are worth 15% less than comparable properties, with values fully recovering upon remediation — directly relevant to this low-income, ~83-person community. No prior press coverage of this specific undocumented well was found, consistent with its 'lost' status.
